front: What is the question that the p-value asks?
back: If the null hypothesis were true, how likely would it be to see data at least as extreme as what I actually observed, purely by chance? It's a statement about the data, not about the hypothesis — it is NOT the probability that the null hypothesis is true.

The maximum rate for error-free communication over a noisy channel: C = B · log₂(1 + S/N), with C in bits/s, B the bandwidth in Hz, and S/N the linear signal-to-noise ratio. Bandwidth helps linearly, signal power only logarithmically. Below C, coding can make errors arbitrarily rare; above C, reliable communication is impossible.

Reducing the numerical precision of a model's weights (e.g. from 16-bit floats to 8-bit or 4-bit integers) to cut memory use and speed up inference. A 70B model needs ~140 GB at FP16 but only ~35 GB at 4-bit — the difference between a server rack and a single GPU. Costs a small quality loss: usually negligible at 8-bit, noticeable but often acceptable at 4-bit.

A hypothetical megastructure: a vast cloud of independent satellites or solar collectors orbiting a star to capture a large fraction of its energy output. It's the realistic version of the 'Dyson sphere' — a rigid shell would be mechanically impossible, so Freeman Dyson actually envisioned a swarm of free-orbiting elements that can be built incrementally.

front: What is a Type I civilization on the Kardashev scale?
back: A civilization that harnesses all the energy available on its planet — roughly 10¹⁶ W, the sunlight reaching Earth. Type II uses its entire star's output (~10²⁶ W, e.g. via a Dyson swarm); Type III its entire galaxy (~10³⁶ W). Humanity is currently around 0.7 (~10¹³ W).

A floating-point operation — one arithmetic operation (add, multiply, …) on floating-point numbers, the basic unit of computational work. FLOPs as a count measures total compute (training a frontier LLM ≈ 10²⁵–10²⁶ FLOPs); FLOPS or FLOP/s as a rate measures hardware speed (a modern GPU ≈ 10¹⁵ FLOP/s, a petaflop).

front: What is the half-life of a drug, and why is it a useful measure?
back: The time for the drug's plasma concentration to fall by half. Elimination is usually exponential (a constant fraction removed per unit time), so the half-life is independent of dose — one number predicts all the timing: dosing interval, time to steady state under repeated dosing (~4–5 half-lives), and washout after stopping (~97% gone after 5 half-lives).

When a technology makes using a resource more efficient, total consumption of that resource often rises rather than falls: the efficiency gain lowers the effective cost, and demand expands enough to more than offset the per-unit saving. Named after William Stanley Jevons, who noted in 1865 that more efficient steam engines increased Britain's total coal use. Modern echoes: more fuel-efficient cars encourage more driving; cheaper lighting expands total lighting. It's why efficiency alone doesn't guarantee lower resource use — you often also need a price signal (e.g. a tax) to curb the rebound.

The time-extended version of 3D Gaussian Splatting. In 3D splatting a scene is stored as millions of tiny 3D Gaussians — fuzzy blobs each with a position, size, orientation, color, and opacity — that are rasterized to render photorealistic novel viewpoints in real time (a fast alternative to NeRF-style neural fields). The '4D' adds time as a dimension so the Gaussians can move and deform, letting the method reconstruct and replay dynamic scenes (video / moving subjects) rather than just a static snapshot, while keeping real-time rendering.
